coredata.dc:description: Materials for energy applications, in particular for conversion and storage of energy, have received an upsurge of interest. Materials of this kind require zero-toxicity, abundance, low-cost, robustness in uses, chemical stability, tunability of properties and ease of preparation to ensure their large scale applications. Carbon dots (C-dots) by virtue of their inherent properties appear to be the most promising material in this regard. C-dots comprising discrete, quasispherical particles with sizes below 10 nm have first been discovered in 2004. Since then research experiences numerous attempts to explore the unique properties of C-dots and to exploit them for energy applications. In this review, we report significant advances of this emerging family of carbon materials in three major fields of energy such as conversion of solar energy into electricity, catalytic splitting of water into hydrogen fuel and storage of electrical energy in supercapacitors and provide an in-depth analysis to highlight their prospects in future applications. At a juncture of global energy crisis, attempts have been made to establish a common venue for researchers working on C-dots for different energy applications. Finally, we address critical insights with the aim towards inspiring more exciting work on C-dots as nano-modules for new energy applications.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as nano-modules for energy conversion and storage

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Dhaka, Dhaka 1000, Bangladesh

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Dhaka, Dhaka 1000, Bangladesh

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Materials for energy applications, in particular for conversion and storage of energy, have received an upsurge of interest. Materials of this kind require zero-toxicity, abundance, low-cost, robustness in uses, chemical stability, tunability of properties and ease of preparation to ensure their large scale applications. Carbon dots (C-dots) by virtue of their inherent properties appear to be the most promising material in this regard. C-dots comprising discrete, quasispherical particles with sizes below 10 nm have first been discovered in 2004. Since then research experiences numerous attempts to explore the unique properties of C-dots and to exploit them for energy applications. In this review, we report significant advances of this emerging family of carbon materials in three major fields of energy such as conversion of solar energy into electricity, catalytic splitting of water into hydrogen fuel and storage of electrical energy in supercapacitors and provide an in-depth analysis to highlight their prospects in future applications. At a juncture of global energy crisis, attempts have been made to establish a common venue for researchers working on C-dots for different energy applications. Finally, we address critical insights with the aim towards inspiring more exciting work on C-dots as nano-modules for new energy applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Modern civilization relies on the production, storage, and use of energy on a large scale. The ever increasing global demand necessitates production of usable energy and extraction of prodigious energy stores. It is crucial that the efficient storage and distribution would minimize system loss arisen to offset between demand and supply of energy. Smart protocols are badly needed for production and storage of energy through the preferable use of non-toxic, cheap and environmentally-benign materials. The search of suitable energy-related materials and exploration of their prospects have thus been the key challenges to the material scientists, engineers, and technologists.

Eyes have recently been pointed on the use of the family of quasi-0D carbon-based materials, known as carbon dots (C-dots). Owing to their non-toxic nature, abundance and low-cost, C-dots have been recognized as fascinating materials for environmental and energy applications ranging from chemical catalysis, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis to energy storage such as batteries and capacitors. C-dots comprise discrete and quasispherical particles with sizes below 10 nm [1]. Synthesis of C-dots is rather simple; both a top-down approach, for instance, oxidative cutting of larger graphitic carbons and a bottom-up approach like carbonization of molecular precursors can be used. The unique physicochemical properties, which inter alia include: electron donor and acceptor behavior, and optical, and electrical properties have made C-dots intriguing in optronics, catalysis and sensors. Consideration of solubility, robust chemical inertness, facile modification and high resistance to photobleaching makes C-dots superior to other materials of this kind. The rich photoluminescence (PL) and photochemical properties also make C-dots efficient materials such as photocatalysts for selective oxidation, light-driven acid-catalysis and hydrogen bond catalysis [2]. They have also been very active additives commonly used in energy devices, e.g., solar cells [3], photoelectronic water splitting cells [4], batteries [5] and supercapacitors [6,7] for improved performances. Size-dependent optical absorption and multiple exciton generation effects have rendered C-dots as ideal light absorbers in mesoscopic solar cells.

On the basis of structural properties, C-dots can be classified into three categories, (a) carbon dots: amorphous quasi-spherical nano-dots (1–20 nm), absence of quantum confinement effect (QCE), i.e., band gap is somewhat independent of its size, (b) carbon quantum dots (CQDs): crystalline quasi-spherical nanoparticle (1–20 nm), exhibit a moderate QCE, i.e., band gap is dependent of its size and (c) graphene quantum dots (GQDs): several layers of 0D-graphene sheets (1–10 nm), exhibit a strong QCE, i.e., band gap is strongly dependent of its size [1,3].

C-dots are basically electrochemically inert, while their electrochemical properties, such as, band gap and redox potentials exhibit tunability dependent character. Changing the synthetic conditions lead to their incorporation in a number of different energy harvesting or photocatalytic applications. This has generated a lot of interest and recent literature has experienced certain improvements and modulation of properties, for instances, involving graphitization and N-doping [8–10].

One of the most salient features of C-dots is the QCE. Due to the QCE, C-dots experience increased band gap energy and a blue shift in their spectral response. The band gap energy of particles increases with the decreasing size, whereas it decreases by heteroatom doping, and surface fabrication [11]. These facts provide a privilege to design C-dots with a broader spectral response from ultraviolet (UV) to near infrared (NIR) region that actually make C-dots as a compatible candidate in solar photovoltaics (PV). Owing to the higher surface area and surface edge defect, C-dots provide more tunneling channels for the movement of the electron and thus suppress the electron-hole recombination. All of these add advantages to the PV in generating excess charge carriers and accelerating their dynamics over the electrical circuit.

Another important property of C-dots is that they can easily couple with other semiconducting photocatalysts using organic functional groups acting as photosensitizers [12–14]. Combining with other semiconducting photocatalysts C-dots broaden their light absorption range in UV–visible region and can transfer photoexcited electrons to the contacted acceptor [15]. C-dots have thus been considered as the suitable alternative to toxic, expensive photosensitizers, such as, organic dyes, metal oxides and, metal nanoparticles in fabrication of solar cell devices.

Carbon-based materials, due to their favorable properties such as low-cost, light weight, simple processability, electrochemical inertness, and structural tenability, are the predominant component in electric double layer (EDL) capacitors called supercapacitors electrodes [7]. Several concepts have so far been materialized in the recent years to introduce C-dots in supercapacitors. C-dots may be converted into high-surface-area carbon electrodes and the capacitance and electrical conductivity of electrodes based on carbon, metal compounds, and/or conducting polymers may be improved. In addition, C-dots assist to avoid the restacking of other components, improve porosity and ion diffusion, and enhance electron transport within the electrode materials. Due to their characteristic small size, they have intrinsically high surface areas and have the potential to interact with a variety of other electrode materials and to ensure high surface area and pseudocapacitive sites [16].

For the sake of a modern civilization, energy issues should be addressed with focus on the energy materials. The state of materials explored needs to be benchmarked to foster further development in a smooth and rapid manner. In this review, we provide some in-depth discussion and understanding on the most promising energy material, C-dots and their properties and recent progress on their applications in the field of energy conversion and storage such as conversion of solar energy into electricity, catalytic splitting of water into hydrogen gas fuel and storage of electrical energy in supercapacitors. The article provides some critical insights to stimulate further exciting works on C-dots as nano-modules for new energy applications.

Facile syntheses of C-dots from different renewable precursors have been attempted ( Table 1). As stated above C-dots with different structures and properties can be in general synthesized by two widely used synthetic approaches, i.e., top-down and bottom-up ( Fig. 1). The top-down approach is cited as the breakdown of larger carbon structures such as graphite, graphene, carbon nanotube, fullerene, coal, carbon black, and so on, into smaller carbon moieties under intense and severe conditions, i.e., laser ablation, mechanical milling, chemical oxidation, electrochemical oxidation, arc discharge, and ultrasonic-assisted methods. While the bottom-up approach is the formation of C-dots starting from a relatively small carbon moiety under a mild condition such as hydrothermal synthesis, microwave-assisted carbonization, plasma treatment, pyrolysis, and ultrasonic reaction [17]. The size and crystallinity of C-dots solely depend on their synthetic routes and reaction environment, e.g., top-down approach generally produces highly crystalline C-dots, while the C-dots obtained from the bottom-up approach reveals an amorphous structure with a higher number of doping sites and surface functional groups.

The post processing is also crucial to develop desired properties of C-dots. It has been reported that C-dots synthesized by the top-down approach show a short persistent PL with a low quantum yield, while products from the bottom-up method exhibit a pronounced PL property with a high quantum yield. By particular post synthesis treatment, PL and photocatalytic activities of C-dots can be enhanced by passivating their surface structures with various negatively charged polar moieties, such as, −CO, −COOH, −OH, −NH2, −CONH2, etc. In this case, an in situ N-doping results in the improved PL and bathochromic or red-shifted absorption that are advantageous for the solar energy conversion device. Moreover, tuning the properties via main synthetic routes or post synthesis method by introducing several oxygen and nitrogen containing groups, pseudocapacitance in the C-dots can be developed. Amorphous nature, higher surface area, electrical conductivity and so on make C-dots as a suitable candidate for supercapacitor application.

In summary, among several synthesis strategies, the favorable one is fundamentally selected based on the comparative feasibility, targeted application, and the desired properties. For example, the products from the top-down approach are easy to produce, simple, and have well architectural structure, whereas the products from the bottom-up approach are difficult to produce due to the formation of various side products along with C-dots [18]. Therefore, synthetic route of this kind requires meticulous reaction handling and a deliberate effort for the purification of the product. Nevertheless, in the bottom-up approach, the surface passivation and doping are much easier via ‘one-pot synthesis’ approach without the requirement of any post synthetic chemical treatment. Considering these issues, researchers are more devoted to the production of C-dots from several natural renewable precursors such as, corn powder, banana pill, glucose, chitin, etc., and to use these C-dots in various task specific applications as summarized in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots and conversion of solar energy
ce:section.ce:para: Conversion of sunlight into electrical energy is an alternative energy source that is renewable, eco-friendly, and efficacious for reducing greenhouse gas in the atmosphere. Solar cell is the emerging module for the conversion of sunlight into electricity. Before discussing the crucial role of C-dots in constructing this emerging technology, the fundamentals and mechanistic of solar cells are described in a nutshell here. The epoch of the solar cell is sorted into three sections 1st, 2nd and 3rd generation solar cell. The 1st generation solar cell encompasses silicone chips or a single crystal of silicon. It is the widely used solar cell technology and has reached the zenith of its advanced research although it suffers from the drawback of high expense and lower photoconversion efficiency (PCE). The 2nd generation solar cell is the thin-film solar cell, where the commonly used materials are CdTe, GaAs and amorphous silicon. The limited PCE of thin-film solar cells thrives the eyes on the 3rd generation solar cells called dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs).

Nowadays, the propensity of the use of C-dots is aberrantly noted in solid state solar cells, perovskite solar cells, bio-inspired devices, organic sensitized solar cells (OSCs), i.e., bulk heterojunction solar cells, and DSSCs. C-dots can enhance the PCE of the solar photovoltaics due to their unique and beneficial properties including PL, size-dependent QCE associated with changeable band gap energy, and wider absorption edge [41–43]. However, C-dots have been considered well-suited materials for DSSCs for their suitable sensitization, stable electron transport layer with semiconductor metal oxide, and faster charge transport kinetics within this region. Regarding the issue, here, we mainly focus on the role of C-dots as active materials for constructing photoanode and counter electrode (CE) in the voguish and promising energy conversion device, DSSC along with the challenges and future prospects of this underdeveloped solar cell technique.

A DSSC requires three significant steps to convert sunlight into electricity. As depicted in Fig. 2, it comprises four vital parts; the photoanode, sensitizer, mediator, and the CE. Generally, semiconducting oxide materials, e.g., TiO2, ZnO as photoanode, Ru-based inorganic dye N719 as a sensitizer, I‾/I3‾ redox couple as the mediator, and Pt as the CE have been widely used in a DSSC. The mechanism of conversion of electromagnetic radiation into electricity involves initial absorption of light by the dye sensitizer and ejection of electrons from the HOMO to the LUMO, leaving a hole. The ejected electron moves to the conduction band (CB) of the photoanode as the energy of the CB of the photoanode material is lower than the LUMO energy of the dye. This electron reaches the CE through the external circuit. At the 2nd step, the dye species regenerates by accepting an electron from the redox mediator (I‾/I3‾) where I‾ becomes oxidized to I3‾. Finally, I3 ‾ gets reduced by accepting the photogenerated electron in the CE surface [44].

C-dots can be an effective alternative to the conventionally used sensitizer (N719) and CE due to the easy band gap tuning via controlling the size of C-dots, surface functionalization and heteroatom doping in the core structure as described above. Endowing desired band gap allows C-dots the flow of photoejected electron through the CB of semiconductor metal oxide (i.e., TiO2, ZnO) and suppresses the carrier recombination. In addition, C-dots have both the photon up and down conversion effect resulting in a wide spectral absorption and have both the electron donors and acceptors property resulting in a higher number of carrier density in the CB of semiconductor metal oxide. These have been used as co-sensitizer with the N719 dye and augment the cell performance. One, of the major problems associated with the use of organic dye, is their photodegradability under UV illumination, and this issue can be solved by implying the C-dots as sensitizer. The incorporation of C-dots in a 3D polymer structure (PANI, MWCNT) can introduce nucleation in the 3D network owing to their small size and higher specific surface area (SSA), resulting in a composite compete over Pt as a CE in DSSCs. All of these properties associated with the C-dots and their role to enhance the performance of DSSCs are discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots as the photoanode modifier or co-sensitizer in a DSSC
ce:section.ce:para: A crucial issue in the charge transfer process is the recombination of the photogenerated electron, which deteriorates the cell performance. C-dots are an efficacious solution for prohibiting the charge recombination process as they endow an intermediate energy state to accelerate the charge transfer process from the HOMO of the dye to the photoanode. Moreover, it has a higher SSA due to its smaller size (< 10 nm) and can absorb a bounteous amount of dye and hence enhance the photogenerated electron densities when used as a photoanode modifier or co-sensitizer. Furthermore, the material with a higher SSA facilitates the reduction of the I3 ‾ and upholds the PCE of the cell [45].

C-dots show two major absorption peaks at 237 nm and 334 nm, which arise from the π-π* electronic transition of CC and n-π* electronic transition of CO. The light absorption range can be shifted towards the longer wavelength via the surface modification, changing the reaction condition and the precursors of its formation. The commonly used sensitizer N719 dye in a DSSC shows a unique absorption peak at 533 nm, which resembles a limited photon absorption and spectral response of the cell. Therefore, numerous research attempts have been made prior to the modification of the light absorption range of the cell. C-dots, as a co-sensitizer, are a compatible candidate in this race [3,46].

Since the DSSC can convert only the visible portion of light into electricity due to the limited photon absorption of the dye, several studies [19,47] have introduced C-dots both as the energy down and up conversion material to broaden the absorption range from UV to NIR. Teymourinia et al. [47] have introduced GQDs as energy down-conversion (EDC) material in a DSSC for the first time in 2017. As an EDC material, GQDs absorb UV radiation at 360 nm and emits light at 520 nm. The emitted visible light from the GQDs is absorbed by the N719 dye, resulting in higher photogenerated electron density in the CB of TiO2, fast charge carrier separation, and transportation, ultimately leading to the improved light-harvesting ability of the cell. The study has shown 14.8% and 21% enhancement of PCE (η), and short circuit current density (J sc) compared to the cell without GQDs and reported this improvement as the EDC effect of GQDs.

The charge collection or transportation time (τ c) and the charge recombination time (τ r) determined from the intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopy and intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy are very close for both the cell with and without GQD. The charge collection efficiency, η cc= 1– (τ c/τ r), are also very close, 0.98 and 0.99 for both the cell. These values prove that the enhanced PCE has resulted only from the EDC effect of GQDs.

N-doped C-dots (N-C-dots) can also act as both the energy up and down conversion material [19]. The PL spectra of the sample after changing the excitation wavelength (λ ex) has been analyzed. Fig. 3 reveals the up and down converted PL spectra of N-C-dots. When the λ ex gradually increases from 370 to 470 nm, the PL intensity also increases, followed by the red shift and continues up to λ ex = 470 nm. Nevertheless, when the λ ex exceeds over 470 nm, the peak intensity gradually decreases, followed by the blue shifts. The study clearly illustrates that N-C-dots can convert both the UV and NIR radiation into the absorbable visible light for N719 dye. The change of emission wavelength with λ ex may be attributed to the presence of various excitation energy traps originated from the oxygenic groups, such as, −OH, −COOH, −COC− and −CO on the surface of N-C-dots.

The core-shell structure modification of C-dots by a heteroatom, e.g., N-doping, enhances PL intensity resulting in a better EDC property. The enhanced PL is due to the size confinement and conjugation effect in the core of C-dots. The presence of doped N in the core-shell of C-dots leads to the red shift for both the π-π* and n-π* transition as it increases the degree of conjugation followed by the donation of its lone pair electron. This red shift can be further modified by tuning the doping level of Riaz et al. [48] have investigated the N-doping effect in the core of C-dots as EDC material and has found an ameliorate value for J sc (15.02 mA cm−2) and η (8.20%). During the excitation with UV radiation, emission of N-C-dots has been featured from 500 to 550 nm, which is the best absorption range for N719 dye in a DSSC.

The PL property of the C-dots can also be tuned by surface modification along with its emissive trap followed by the different photon retention properties of C-dots [49]. Therefore, C-dots have also energy up conversation property followed by the conversion of NIR radiation to visible range light. The emitted photon from the C-dots can also be reabsorbed by itself and thus enhance the photoinduced electron density in the CB of the photoanode. Thus, C-dots as co-sensitizer in the DSSC broaden their absorption range from UV to NIR endowing superior efficiency.

The efficiency of a DSSC depends on the carrier concentration, electron transportation rate, lifetime of the photoinduced electron, and recombination rate of the charge carriers [20]. The photoinduced electron of the sensitizer can be recombined with its hole or with the I3‾ of the electrolyte by generating the dark current. This dark current reduces the photon to current conversion rate as well as the efficiency of the cell. C-dots suppress the electron recombination rate and enhance the electron transportation rate through the external circuit.

A recent study by Sharif et al. [20] reveal the significant effect of GQDs for the suppression of charge carrier recombination. They fabricated a DSSC where the photoanode has been modified by incorporating GQDs as a co-sensitizer. The GQDs cause extended photon absorption and electron-hole separation resulting in a higher J sc. The transportation behavior of the photogenerated electron has been investigated by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) measurement. The Nyquist plot reveals that the R s and R ct values of TiO2 are 30.17 and 24.30 Ω, whereas for the TiO2/GQDs, these values became 29.80 and 41.98 Ω, respectively. The reduced R s value for the TiO2/GQDs depicts the accelerated photoinduced electron conduction through the photoanode. In contrast, the increased R ct supports the suppression of the photoinduced electron recombination with the I3‾. Meanwhile, the lifetime of electron and the charge collection efficiency in the CB of TiO2 are increased along with the faster charge transport kinetics. Hence, the fabricated DSSC with TiO2/GQDs anode has improved PCE (6.97%), which is 70.83% greater than the DSSC with pristine TiO2 photoanode.

The property of C-dots dramatically changes depending on the precursor sample [19–22,46]. Synthesis of C-dots from the natural resources, for instances, citric acid [46], corn and strawberry powder [19], agro-waste biochar [20], rosemary leaves [21], biomass (glucose, chitin, and chitosan) [22] and so forth, has been the focus of current research.

Padmanathan et al. have modified a photoanode for a DSSC by incorporating C-dots with TiO2 [50], ZnO [51], and WO3 [52]. In each case, the GQDs incorporated photoanode shows a better efficiency than the bare electrode due to the lowering of band gap energy and enhanced SSA of the photoanode material. By using the Kubelka-Munk function from the diffuse reflectance spectroscopy data, the band gap energies for the TiO2, ZnO and WO3 have been calculated as 3.11, 3.13, and 2.63 eV, respectively. After incorporating C-dots, these values are reduced to 2.33, 2.68, and 2.25 eV, respectively. The intensity of the PL spectrum for the composite gradually decreases with the increase in C-dots loading. These results indicate that the photogenerated electron shifts to the CB of the semiconducting oxide rather than a recombination with the hole.

C-dots as the co-sensitizer can manipulate the electron transport pathway from the sensitizer to the outer circuit. Bora et al. [3] have investigated the effect of precursor on the light-emitting ability of the C-dots. They have synthesized the green light-emitting C-dots (G-CDs) from the citric acid precursor, which absorbs light around 422 nm, and used C-dots as the co-sensitizer in a DSSC. The cell efficiency is 6.90% that is larger than the cell without using C-dots, 6.28%. The enhanced PCE has been thoroughly investigated. The HOMO and LUMO energy state of the G-CDs calculated from the cyclic voltammograms (CV) are − 5.3 and − 3.1 eV, respectively. The HOMO and LUMO energy of the N719 and the CB (−4.3 eV) of the TiO2 illustrate that there is a thermodynamically favorable pathway for electron transportation from the LUMO (−3.1 eV) of G-CDs to the LUMO (−3.9 eV) of N719 and then the CB (−4.3 eV) of TiO2. Due to the absorption of visible light, G-CDs also eject electrons and contribute with the photoinduced electrons of N719 to increase the PCE of the cell. The incorporated energy levels of G-CDs also accelerate electron transportation by preventing the electron-hole recombination in the N719 dye. Interestingly, the alignment of energy levels also endows a favorable path for the photoinduced hole transportation from the HOMO (−5.4 eV) of N719 to the HOMO (−5.3 eV) of G-CDs, which facilitates the oxidation of I‾ as well as the I3‾ reduction at the CE.

An analogous result has been reported by Jahantigh et al. [53], where they have fabricated a DSSC with a single-layer GQDs (SL-GQDs) incorporated TiO2 photoanode. The incorporation of C-dots modifies the UV–vis absorption band to a great extent. Fig. 4b shows a strong absorption peak for SL-GQDs/N719 than the pristine N719. It depicts the potential applicability of C-dots as light-absorbing material. When the anode is co-sensitized with SL-GQD, the value of J sc and V oc increases significantly (Fig. 4c), which indicates an improved PCE of the fabricated DSSC. The improved values are due to the fast charge transfer kinetics in the SL-GQD-N719/TiO2 compared to the N719/TiO2 photoanode, followed by a thermodynamically favorable charge transfer mechanism as illustrated in Fig. 5a.

EIS spectrum of SL-GQD-N719/TiO2 gives strong evidence for the faster charge transfer kinetics. When the conventionally used photoanode is co-sensitized with SL-GQD, the R ct value attenuates significantly due to the superior charge extraction, transportation, and charge carrier suppression. The introduction of SL-GQDs into TiO2 also increases the SSA as more N719 dye can be adsorbed, and hence contributes to the enhanced photoelectron generation. Close contact of the TiO2 and C-dots raises a region of faster charge carrier transport and improves the stability of the cell. The semiconducting oxide material, e.g., TiO2, ZnO, SnO2 may interact with the ligands of the Ru dye and also dissolve in the electrolyte solution. Incorporation of C-dots can prevent this dissolution process and the aggregation of dye species by the repulsive interaction between the surface functional groups, e.g., −OH, −COOH, −NH2, etc. and the ligand of the N719 dye.

All the previous studies have shown that C-dots co-sensitized photoanode have an excellent PCE. However, a higher amount of loading erodes the performance of the cell due to the creation of new surface defects. Instead of blocking the recombination trap, excess C-dots loading introduces new recombination sites and slows down the carrier mobility. It may also aggregate due to the van der Waals and π-π stacking interaction. However, heteroatom, e.g., N [19], B [54] doping, can solve this issue by creating a lattice space between the adjacent C-dots and by providing a higher SSA.

The use of C-dots itself as a sensitizer [24] has not been reported to satisfy the desired performance of a solar cell. As C-dots have a narrow absorption band and a probable larger number of recombination traps, they are not promising to replace an inorganic dye. There is also a weak affinity between C-dots and TiO2, resulting in lower stability. They cannot compete with the metal to ligand charge transfer process of the dye. Instead, they can reinforce the charge transfer process by providing the photon of suitable energy used as a co-sensitizer.

The central role of CE in DSSC is to collect photogenerated electron from the outer circuit and to catalyze the reduction reaction of I3 ‾ (I3 ‾ + 2e‾ = 3I ‾). A study by Zhang et al. [45] has shown that the reduction reaction of I3‾ occurs at an adsorbed state on the CE surface. Therefore, the material to be used as CE in a DSSC has to fulfill two criteria; superior electrical conductivity and intriguing electrocatalytic activity. Pt is the best fit for that and is conventionally used in the DSSC model. Nevertheless, it is highly precious and increases the cell production cost. Hence, there is a surge of interest to synthesize a practical, high-performance CE to replace Pt and carbon-based materials are the promising candidate in this research field.

Among the carbon family members, C-dots are promising for their higher SSA and enriched electron density in their defect sites as they can accelerate the reduction of I3‾. The defects of C-dots originate from their finite size. A higher ratio of edge to basal carbon atoms, surface functionalities, easy tunability, and the introduction of more defects or emissive trap sites by element doping promote their use as CE in a DSSC. Despite all of these, they still have a deteriorated performance as compared to Pt. But some studies have shown that when C-dots are used as a modifier or tailoring agent, as a component of a composite, the tailored material shows an ameliorate performance over Pt [55].

A comprehensive study by Chang et al. has shown an excellent means of fabrication of C-dots to enhance their performance towards the I3‾ reduction [55]. Due to the higher defect densities, C-dots show good electrocatalytic activity but lower electrical conductivity. By balancing these two factors, they proposed a model where C-dots are incorporated on the 2D carbon surface (CS), ultimately increasing the conjugation of carbon atoms. Flat hexagonal-shaped CS with sp 2-hybridized carbon atoms shows a higher electrical conductivity but lower catalytic activity. However, incorporating C-dots on the CS exposes more defect-rich active edge carbon sites, significantly increasing the electrocatalytic activity by endowing more adsorption sites for I2. The conductivity of the C-dots/CS composite also increases due to the generation of the longer periodicity of carbon atoms and the π-π conjugation network. This enlarged conjugation also increases the electron density at the CE surface and fasten the I3‾ reduction kinetics. A density-functional theory calculation also shows lower I2 adsorption energy for this system, a reliable indication for the potential uses of C-dots as CE in a DSSC.

Defect densities of C-dots can be tuned by incorporating another species and changing its surface functionalities. They can also modulate the structure of the incorporated substance as they can tightly bind the metal ions of positive charge by the negatively charged functional groups on the surface. However, when C-dots are combined with another molecule, they exhibit a better performance due to synergistic effect [56].

CE in a DSSC is illuminated only from the front side but not from the dark or rare side. A modern bifacial solar cell that can harvest solar energy from the both sides has attracted much attention as it can upgrade the overall cell performance [26]. In this solar cell system, C-dots based CE endows an extra electron density at the electrode surface. Being a metal cluster, Pt is not optically transparent, and not recommended in a bifacial solar cell. C-dots incorporated highly transparent CoSe shows a featured performance in this regard. Upon illumination from the rare side, tandem C-dots absorb a photon, become excited, and eject an electron from its HOMO to LUMO, and ultimately provide an electron enriched CE surface (Fig. 5b).

−NH2 as a surface functional group in GQDs can donate its lone pair electron density towards the electro reduction of I3‾. The maximized number of oxygenic and sulfate groups produced during the oxidation process of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) slows down the electron transport kinetics. When it is reduced to r-GOQDs, it shows better catalytic activity and electrochemical stability. Pt degrades with time because of its slow dissolution process in the I‾/I3‾ electrolyte. Unlike Pt, r-GOQDs is electrochemically stable towards I2 within the potential window of operation of a DSSC. The smaller size of r-GOQDs with a higher number of active sites also plays a vital role in the catalytic reduction of I3‾ [57].

Several studies [25,59] have been conducted with the conductive polymer as a benign CE material. However, their performance is not comparable with the Pt due to their lower electrical conductivity. The organic conductive polymers agglomerate due to the strong π-π interaction and van der Waals force in their individual chain. The agglomeration also reduces the active surface area for the I2 adsorption.

C-dots can easily incorporate into the polymer network due to their comparatively small size to prevent the agglomeration of the individual chain. By introducing the nucleation process, C-dots can also tailor the growth of the polymer network. Lee et al. [25] have synthesized polyaniline and C-dots composite (PANI/C-dots) as an efficient CE for a DSSC. They have loaded different amounts of C-dots into PANI network structure to investigate the effect of C-dots loading on the SSA of the synthesized composite. Fig. 6b and c show that both the porosity and the surface area of the PANI composite increase with the increased C-dots loading. The porosity and SSA of the composite increase up-to 5 wt% of C-dots loading. Pristine PANI shows the agglomerated structure (Fig. 6a) due to van der Waals, and π-π stacking interaction within its network ring structure. When C-dots are incorporated into PANI network, the agglomeration of PANI is hindered, and the PANI that is formed via nucleation on C-dots exhibits a highly defined and porous structure. This is due to the fact that C-dots contain various negatively charged sites on their surface that repel each other and the electron-rich −NH2 functional group of PANI and thus, obscure the π-π stacking interaction of the PANI ring and increases the SSA of the composite. However, when the loading amount exceeds over 5 wt%, the excessive nuclei of C-dots aggregate within themselves (Fig. 6d) and the SSA of the composite reduces drastically.

The CV curves with larger reduction current density and low potential separation between the oxidation and reduction peaks denote a better catalytic performance for the I3‾ reduction. CVs ( Fig. 7a) show the current densities at the cathodic peaks, which have been used to evaluate the electrocatalytic activity of CEs. The current densities on CEs based on PANI, and PANI/C-dots are − 6.18 and − 7.66 mA cm−2, respectively. The current density value for PANI/C-dots is higher than the pristine PANI. Besides, the CE of PANI/C-dots exhibits better peak-to-peak voltage separation (E pp) compared to that of the PANI CE. This improvement in the peak current densities and the E pp value of the PANI/C-dots electrode indicates the enhanced catalytic activity to facilitate the I3 ‾ reduction.

The PANI/C-dots composite shows lower R ct (Fig. 7b), higher exchange current density, and higher limiting diffusion current density (Fig. 7c) that clarify the better electrical conductivity and catalytic activity towards I3‾ reduction than the PANI composite. The PCE of the cell increases from 5.60% to 7.45% when C-dots are incorporated into the PANI network, resulting from the synergistic effect of improved electrical conductivity and surface roughness. This PCE value is comparable to the Pt CE (7.37%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots as a catalyst in the field of photocatalytic H2 generation
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned before, C-dots are non-toxic, stable, abundant, inexpensive, and have high photocatalytic activity, which lead them to use as a photocatalyst in photocatalytic water-splitting [4]. Photocatalytic H2 production using sunlight is a zero-carbon emission green source of energy and the search of green and renewable energy has made this one of the most exploited fields over the past few decades. Photocatalytic water splitting involves light-irradiated catalysts to produce H2 and O2 from H2O. Combined with other photocatalysts, C-dots can change surface morphology, increase surface area and modify the band gap to make them interesting for use as a co-catalyst. Before discussing the specific roles of C-dots in catalytic splitting of H2O, the fundamentals regarding energy associated with this chemical reaction as well as the function of photon on the surface of catalyst have been described in brief.

The successful visible light-induced water splitting processes follow one of the two approaches. The first approach considers a single visible light responsive photocatalyst to split water via a single one-step excitation mechanism ( Fig. 8a) [65,66]. In this case, the change in Gibbs free energy (∆G 0) is positive, meaning the reaction is thermodynamically unfavorable.
(1) H2O = H2 + ½O2 ∆G 0 = 237·13 kJ mol−1

Only a semiconductor photocatalyst with a sufficient band gap energy can drive this reaction via accepting photons containing equal or greater energy than its band gap. In general, the photons excite electrons from the VB to the CB of the photocatalyst, leaving holes behind. If the bottom of the CB is located at a more negative potential than H+/H2 potential (0 V vs. NHE at pH = 0), the photogenerated electrons can reduce H+ into H2, whereas the holes can oxidize H2O into O2 if the top of the VB is located at a more positive potential than H2O/O2 potential (1.23 V vs. NHE at pH = 0) [67]. To harvest more visible light and increase solar to hydrogen efficiency, the band should be as narrow as possible. However, narrowing the band gap can decrease the driving force that compensates for the energy barrier associated with the redox reaction.

The other approach is governed by a two-step excitation mechanism utilizing two photocatalysts. The mechanism is called the Z-scheme overall water splitting [68,69]. A H2 evolution photocatalyst, an O2 evolution photocatalyst, and a shuttle redox mediator are essential to construct a symbolic Z-scheme (Fig. 8b). The photogenerated electrons at the conduction band of H2 evolution photocatalyst reduce H+ to H2, and the photogenerated holes at the valence band oxidize the reductive mediator. While at the O2 evolution photocatalyst, photogenerated electrons at the CB oxidize the as-reduced mediator to the reduced form, and the photogenerated holes at the VB oxidize H2O to O2. Photocatalysts having either a conduction band minimum above the H+/H2 potential or valence band maximum below the H2O/O2 potential can be used to construct the Z-scheme. A new wide range of narrow band gap materials can act as photocatalysts in this scheme. Due to their narrow band gap, a large portion of visible light can generate electrons and holes and drive the overall photocatalytic water splitting.

Separation of photogenerated holes and electrons, rapid charge migration to the surface sites without recombination or trapping, and surface catalytic reactions of adsorbed species and photogenerated charges to produce H2 and O2 are all essential steps that dictate photocatalyst performance in both mechanisms. All steps above depend on many factors such as crystallinity, crystal structure, crystal size, surface structure, surface morphology and surface area of the catalyst. In the following sections, catalytic roles of pristine C-dots and as co-catalyst in composites with other semiconducting materials are systematically described.

In composite photocatalysts of C-dots with semiconducting materials, C-dots enhance the photon absorption of the composite in UV–visible range as C-dots show strong absorption band in the UV–visible range. The photo-induced electron at CB of C-dots can be easily transferred to the CB of acceptor semiconducting material and this prevents the electron-hole pairs recombination. Through prevention of electron-hole pairs recombination and generation of charge carrier (electrons and holes), C-dots improves the photocatalytic activity of the composite [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pristine carbon dots as a photocatalyst in photocatalytic H2 generation
ce:section.ce:para: Yang et al. [4] have carried out photocatalytic water-splitting reactions of water both in the presence and absence of a sacrificial agent. In the presence of pristine C-dots as photocatalyst, the H2 evolution rate is more significant in the presence of methanol as the holes sacrificing donor than that of in pure water ( Fig. 9a). UV radiation has been used as an energy source. Pristine C-dots also show prominent photostability (Fig. 9b). However, under visible light irradiation, no H2 has been detected. Due to this immense band gap energy of C-dots (4.88 eV), visible light cannot excite electrons from VB to CB. This limits the use of pure C-dots as a photocatalyst in visible light driven water splitting process.

A material with suitable band gap energy, higher SSA, optimum operational potential window and longtime cyclability is required to be a photocatalyst for water splitting process. The widely used materials for photocatalytic water splitting are TiO2 [70], ZnO [71], ZnS [72], CdS [73], CdSe [74], and WO3 [75]. But problems associated with these conventional semiconducting materials are their wide band gap energies and inappropriate surface module for the adsorption of the H2O. C-dots can play a significant role in dealing with these issues as they possess higher SSA, tunable intermediate energy state and different surface defects. Therefore, attempts have been made to use C-dots as co-photocatalyst with other conventional photocatalyst materials.

Kim et al. [28] have fabricated C-dots modified ZnO photoanode (C-dots/ZnO), where C-dots have been used as a co-photocatalyst. ZnO is considered an efficacious photoelectrode material due to its enlarged photoresponsibility, faster electron mobility, and abundance. However, pristine ZnO can only absorb light in the UV region due to its wide band gap energy. When C-dots are incorporated in pristine ZnO, a stable electrode is formed, which shows spectral response around 500 nm in the visible light region. The C-dots/ZnO nanostructure photoelectrodes are also very stable as the amine passivation of the C-dots can remove surface defect states and constitute a chemical shield, protecting their core from undesirable oxidation. The hydrogen bond formation enhances the adsorption of C-dots on the ZnO surface with the ZnO via the hydroxyl groups present in the C-dots surface, which are formed during the synthesis of C-dots. The cyclability of the photoelectrode has been reported to be higher than that constructed with pristine ZnO. The incorporation of C-dots in ZnO increases the electron density in the CB and hole density in the VB as C-dots have suitable VB and CB positions as depicted in Fig. 10a. The excessive electrons in the CB facilitate the reduction process of H+ to H2 and the generated hole accelerates the oxidation of H2O to O2. The fabricated C-dots/ZnO photoanode shows four-times more current density (0.72 mA cm−2) than that of the bare ZnO photoelectrode (0.18 mA cm−2). Finally, the C-dots/ZnO photoanode exhibits 93% Faradic efficiency, indicating the potential use of C-dots as the co-photocatalyst.

The incorporation of C-dots loading also changes the absorption response of the ZnO. C-dots/ZnO can show absorption (Fig. 10b) of visible light, where ZnO absorbs light only in the UV region. It can generate photoexcited electrons under light irradiation and separate electron-hole pairs with a cascade band structure using ZnO, and the cascade band structure enables efficient charge transfer between C-dots and ZnO nanostructures through their interface.

An exciting work has been reported by Liu et al. [76]. They have synthesized C-dots/C3N4 efficient photocatalyst for water splitting via a two-electron pathway where C-dots are embedded into the C3N4 matrix. Photocatalysts with sulfide and nitride are so unstable that they show more oxidation tendency than H2O under the operational voltage. Again, in the four-electron H2O oxidation process, the production of O2 requires larger overpotential, and the release of O2 from the catalyst surface is troublesome. To solve both of these emerging problems, they have designed the C-dots/C3N4 photocatalyst to fasten the reaction kinetics of the two-electron H2O oxidation process than the thermodynamically favorable four-electron H2O oxidation process. In this two electrons H2O oxidation process, firstly, H2O is oxidized via a two-electron pathway to H2O2 (2H2O = H2O2 + H2), and then H2O2 decomposes to O2 and H2O (2H2O2 = 2 H2O + O2). For this, two-electron/two-electron (2e-/2e-) stepwise H2O splitting process, a viable and stable photocatalyst is required to promote the generation of H2 and O2 as well as the decomposition of H2O2 and work in the low overpotential range. Moreover, the synthesized C-dots/C3N4 composite photocatalyst is better than bare C3N4 photocatalyst with a quantum efficiency (QE) of 16%.

During the 2e-/2e- H2O splitting process, C3N4 experiences poisoning by the produced H2O2 and it is difficult to remove them away from the C3N4 surface. C-dots show magnificent catalytic activity to decompose the H2O2 to H2O and O2 because of its unique electron reservoir properties, PL, and photoinduced electron transfer process. Thus, incorporation of C-dots protects the C3N4 catalyst from poisoning effect and increases its cyclability as it can perform for a long time. Fig. 10c illustrates the evolution of H2 and O2 from pure H2O with C-dots/C3N4 photocatalyst loading. H2 and O2 evolution rates are 8.4 and 4.1 μmol h−1, respectively. No H2 and O2 evolution has been detected by gas chromatography when pristine C3N4 and C-dots have been used separately. The evolution of H2 and O2 has only been observed for the C-dots/C3N4 composite photocatalyst. This clearly indicates the necessity of the incorporation of C-dots for the decomposition of H2O2 and the generation of O2. Furthermore, C-dots/C3N4 shows a high H2 and O2 production rate with substantial stability in 200 runs of recycling over 200 days. Thus, C-dots can solve the existing challenges to release diatomic O2 and increase the stability of the C3N4 photocatalyst.

The effect of C-dots loading on the efficiency of C-dots/C3N4 composite photocatalyst has been investigated. The H2 evolution rate is increased by a factor of 5.4 for C-dots/C3N4 composite with a higher concentration of C-dots to be 4.8 × 10−3 g C-dots g−1 catalyst (Fig. 10d). This is due to the increased amount of C-dots loading promoting more sub-band states in the band gap and accelerating the H2O splitting process. Thus, C-dots incorporation as a co-photocatalyst increases the absorbance, stability, QE, and finally, the overall performance of the C-dots/C3N4 photocatalyst for the 2e- pathway of H2O splitting process.

Polyoxometalates (POMs) have attracted much attention as a nano-photocatalysts among researchers. POMs are a family of nanoscale metal oxide polyanionic clusters based on highly oxidized metals. They can reversibly accept a large number of electrons from a donor [77–79] and have shown remarkable catalytic properties [80,81] for water splitting [82,83], which makes them suitable candidates for nano-photocatalysts. However, POMs are active only under UV light.

C-dots can endow POMs with the ability to absorb in the visible region by the action of photosensitization. This is due to the fact that C-dots are highly surface-functionalized nanoparticles and can exhibit strong absorption and bright fluorescence within the visible range [14,84,85]. To produce efficient nanohybrid photocatalysts, Madonia et al. [86] have used C-dots as sensitizers for POMs. They have chosen the Dawson-structured [P2W18O62]6- metal oxide cluster [87] for its ability to accept a large number of electrons in the development of POM-C-dots hybrid systems. The study has been conducted with both types of C-dots depending on their surface charge; negative (C-dots-; pH 10) and positive (C-dots+; pH 4).

Both negative (at pH 10) and positive (at pH 4) C-dots show a strong absorption at the blue end of the visible spectrum with a better absorption edge for C-dots+ reaching up to 500 nm ( Fig. 11a). Estimated emission quantum yields also exhibits better results for C-dots+ (36%) than C-dots− (11%) when excited at 400 nm. These differences bear the indication of the involvement of surface chemical groups in the optical transitions, which are expected to favor the electron transfer to POMs.

Fig. 11b shows that upon adding POMs into C-dots solutions, fluorescence quenching is way greater for C-dots+ solution, e.g., 75% at a concentration of POMs as low as 5.8 μM, than C-dots− solution. Further study of quenching at several POMs concentration proves the quenching is static [86]. These results indicate the formation of stable POM--C-dots+ complexes by electrostatic interaction, and the quenching occurs within the POM--C-dots+ complexes by efficient transfer of photoinduced electrons from C-dots+ to POMs. So, C-dots can be used as photosensitizers for POMs, and those hybrids are useful in the photocatalytic process, such as water splitting.

Composites of C-dots with other semiconducting materials exhibit better H2 evolution rate than many other photocatalytic materials investigated recently. Furthermore, C-dots improve the H2 evolution rate of the composite photocatalysts. Comparison between C-dots and few conventional photocatalysts are summarized in
Tables 3 and 4.

A supercapacitor (SC) is a charge storage electrochemical device that combines the properties of conventional capacitors and batteries into one single device. They are fast in action like capacitors with longer life span but with higher energy density like batteries. SCs adopt two mechanistic ways of storing electrical charges on the surface of the electrode, one is electric double layer capacitance (EDLC) that is electrostatic in origin and the other is pseudocapacitance that is electrochemical in nature [99]. The electrode material is the primal component that determines the mechanism of charge storage hence the capacitive performance of any SC. There lies a whole spectrum of materials with various attractive features that can be employed as electrode materials. Carbonaceous materials, such as activated carbons (AC), carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene, graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO) etc., have been used as the EDL active material SC [100,101] because of their higher electrical conductivity, higher SSA, easier tailorability, low cost, higher mechanical strength, electrochemical inertness and so on. However, there exist some drawbacks with these materials such as, non-uniform pore sizes and non-homogeneous distribution of the pores, low conductivities due to larger pores and porous structure, re-stacking of the nanosheets or tubes resulting in a reduced SSA than theoretically predicted, low energy density etc. limiting their device performance [101]. So it is highly desirable to design a material with narrow pore size, interconnected pore structure or three dimensional structure providing an interconnected conductive network, high SSA for better electrochemical performance. C-dots have attracted a lot of attention as an active electrode material for SC for the past few years. C-dots have the greater potential to enhance the SSA and electrical conductivity of other composites centered on carbon, metal compounds and conducting polymers. By virtue of their smaller size (1–10 nm), they can operate as spacers and reinforcing agents for several layered morphological materials increasing the SSA of the materials and forming crosslink between the layers providing them with extra electrochemical stabilities. Moreover, they are able to dodge the restacking of nanosheets endowing them with more ion transport channels with improved porosities [102]. All of these advantageous features of C-dots can profoundly improve the electrochemical performance, i.e., C sp as well as energy density of SCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots blended with other carbonaceous materials
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots are materials with intrinsically low electrical conductivity with nonzero band gap that can be regulated by tuning size of C-dots. An adverse impact of reducing size on its band gap energy is noticed. With the reduction of size, the band gap energy gets widened resulting in an increase of the bulk resistance as can be demonstrated by scanning tunneling spectroscopy [103]. An effective way to get remedy of this, i.e., to enhance the intrinsic electrical conductivity of C-dots, is to make a composite of C-dots with other carbonaceous materials such as GO, rGO, CNTs etc.

Several research works have been conducted on graphene-based composite electrode materials because of their higher electrical conductivities, larger SSA, higher mechanical strengths and so on. As an ideal electrode material GO is frequently used. A new graphene-based electrode material has been adopted by anchoring C-dots on rGO. The presence of C-dots has improved the specific capacitance (C sp) by 74.3% (211.9 F g−1), which is higher than that of the pristine rGO electrode (121.6 F g−1). Such a drastic enhancement in C sp value has been achieved by mixing C-dots and GO in a ratio of 5:1 and then applying hydrogen plasma reduction. The composite exhibits a superior electrochemical performance with improved cycle stability, a retention of 92.8% of the capacitance of its original value after 1000 cycles [33].

GQDs can be used as spacers and can be inserted between the layers of GO nanosheets ( Fig. 12c). This insertion increases interlayer spacing and enlarges the surface area of GO preventing the agglomeration of GO nanosheets and provides more ion channels for the ion diffusion. Xu. et al. [104] have photo-reduced a mixture of GO and GQDs in ammonia atmosphere and synthesized N-rGO/GQDs, where GQDs act as spacer between GO nanosheets as well as the initial nucleation sites for controllable N-doping at graphene basal planes. N-rGO/GQDs with cross-linked nanosheets exhibits a high nitrogen content of 18.86% with a C sp of 344 F g−1 at a current density of 0.25 A g−1 in 6 M KOH. N-rGO/GQDs shows a better electrochemical performance than N-rGO and rGO (Fig. 12a, b).

Electrospun carbon nanofibers (ECNFs) have attracted much attention over the recent years due to its structural and functional integration characteristics. But one crucial limitation of ECNFs is their SSA which is often less than 1000 m2. This results in a very poor C sp which is typically less than 200 F g−1. Introduction of pores, physically or chemically, can enlarge the SSA. But it seriously damages the continuous carbon skeleton and causes a drastic fall in overall electrical conductivity. To address these issues, activated GQDs have been introduced to ECNF to prepare activated GQD-reinforced ECNF (AGRCNF) composite. In this composite, GQDs play the bifunctional role of constructing an entire reinforcing phase and conductive network due to their strong cross-linking effect (cross-linked C−O−C bonds) and high crystallinity. The highly conductive, porous GQD embedded ECNF, AGRCNF-3 (3:1 ratio of KOH and carbon precursor), with enlarged SSA ( Fig. 13a) and sufficient ion migration channels (Fig. 13b) exhibit an excellent electrochemical performance with remarkably high C sp values of 335 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 150 F g−1 at 500 A g−1. Moreover, the device can be charged to 80% in just 2.2 s [105].

A similar effect has been reported with AC by Yan et al. [34] ACs are the most commonly used material for commercially available SCs because of their low cost and high SSA. But lower energy density, lower operating voltage window (OPW) restricts their practical application.2 Introduction of pores on AC can solve this problem to some extent but it also diminishes the conductive network of AC. As a result, the electrolytic ion transport and storage are severely disturbed in the deep and branched micropores. These issues have been checked by Yan et al. [106] by developing an overall conductive network inside the activated carbon via embedding highly crystallized GQDs. Under an optimized condition, 2829 m2 g−1 SSA has been achieved for the microporous carbon with C sp value of 388 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 with an improved performance and cycle stability. Therefore, embedding highly crystallized GQDs on AC formed conductive networks inside AC skeleton significantly improves the charge-transfer kinetics and ion migration rate into the deep and branched micropores. Similarly, additional redox active sites, conductive network etc. have improved the C sp of CNT/GQD by 200% compared with the bare CNT electrodes.

Uniform deposition of GQDs onto the 3D graphene (3DG) scaffolds introduces additional pores, improves SSA of 3DG from 192 to 292 m2 g−1 and enhances the electrical conductivity of 3DG from 0.35 to 3.70 S m−1 which eventually result in improved C sp. GQDs deposited 3DG exhibits a high C sp of 268 F g−1 with 90% improvement over the bare 3DG electrodes (136 F g−1) [107].

The materialistic superiority of C-dots as compared with other carbon based materials such as GO, rGO, CNF, CNT and so on is apparent. Intrinsically, they have greater SSA, specific capacitance, energy and power density than other carbon based materials. This gives C-dots greater potential for use as an electrode material. Moreover, they have unique ability to modify the other carbonaceous materials making them useful for SC application.

One of the main drawbacks of a SC is its lower energy density. Fundamentally, the combination EDL capacitor with pseudocapacitance can be an antidote for that [108]. GQDs can serve as a pseudocapacitive material providing active reaction sites [102]. Doping of GQDs with N, B etc. can commence pseudocapacitive activities in the system. In a typical example, N-doped GQDs have been electrochemically deposited onto the metal organic framework of carbonized zeolitic imidazolate framework (cZIF-8) decorated CNT composites N-GQD@cZIF-8/CNT. The pseudocapacitive activity due to the N-GQDs can be clearly seen on the following CVs in Fig. 14a, where the two reversible redox peaks are clearly visible. Moreover, the galvanostatic charge discharge (GCD) curve is also distorted from an ideal triangular shape that indicates the combination of EDL and pseudocapacitive energy storage behaviors [109].

Similarly, Zhen Li et al. [35] have prepared a composite with N-GQDs, graphene hydrogel and carbon fibers (N-GQDs/GH/CF). The higher C sp as well as higher energy density are resulted from the pseudocapacitive activity of N-GQDs. The CV of GH/CF has no vivid redox peak and also its integral area is very poor, indicating a lower C sp value and lower energy density. Incorporation of N-GQDs onto the GH/CF composite enhances the C sp value and energy density which is indicated by the enlargement of the integral area of the CV. A pair of reversible redox peaks bear the indication of the pseudocapacitive activity of N-GQDs (Fig. 14b). Moreover, the usage of N-GQDs also preserves the capacitance without sacrificing the cycle stability. After 5000 cycles, the capacitance retention reaches a value of 87.9%, which is a slightly lower than that of the GH/CF electrode (90.2%).

Sulfur doped GQDs (S-GQDs) also contribute to pseudocapacitive behavior enhancing C sp and energy density. In doping, the source of sulfur has been MgSO4.7H2O (sample-I) and CuSO4.7H2O (sample-II) respectively. Superior electrochemical performance of the composites can be confirmed by the quasi-rectangular shape of the CVs and from the distorted triangular GCDs of the sample-I and sample-II, respectively. Presence of S, Cu, and Mg along with rich oxygenated surface of GQDs provides new reactive sites on to the sulfur doped GQDs that induces more surface faradaic reactions [110].

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots blended with transition metal oxides
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of SCs are hindered due to their lower energy density. According to E = 1/2 C × V 2, energy density can be improved either by increasing the OPW or by increasing C sp itself. Improvement of C sp can be achieved using several transition metal oxides (TMO = MnO2, RuO2, Fe3O4, NiCo2O4 etc.). They can assist to do so by their pseudocapacitive characteristics. But they suffer from intrinsically low electrical conductivity, high ion diffusion resistance, low SSA, which limit their further development as an active electrode material for practical purposes [108]. Fusing carbonaceous material with TMOs can go a long way in eradicating these issues. Recently, C-dots have deemed much attention in this regard due to their high electrical conductivity, high SSA, and tunable band gap [111].

In 2013, C-dots based hybrid electrode material has been first reported by Zhu et al. [112]. The reduced C-dots (rC-dots) have been obtained by oxidative exfoliation of graphite material followed by a thermal reduction in the Ar-atmosphere. The desired composite rC-dots/RuO2 has been secured by a sol-gel method followed by the impregnation method. The composite exhibits a superior electrochemical performance where a C sp value of 460 F g−1 has been achieved at an ultrahigh current density of 50 A g−1. Greater rate capability and exceptional cycling stability with 96.9% capacity retention over 5000 cycles at 5 A g−1 have also been achieved (Fig. 14c). Conductive nature of C-dots provides quicker ionic transport during the charge-discharge process and the oxygen containing groups on the surface of C-dots greatly improves the utilization of RuO2 facilitating rapid redox reactions.

Manganese dioxide has been the star of the mystery from the very beginning of the study of the pseudocapacitance because of its facile synthesis, cheap cost, environmental friendliness and higher theoretical capacitance (~1200–1300 F g−1) [142]. That’s why MnO2 has been the center of focus for a very long time. Jia et al. [113] have designed an in situ fabricated GQDs/MnO2 hetero-structural electrode where GQDs lay on the surface of MnO2 nanosheet arrays through the formation of Mn-O-C covalent bond by plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition. Incorporation of GQDs radically improves both the C sp of 1170 F g−1 at 5 mV s−1 and the OPW from 0–1 to 0–1.3 V that can be seen on Fig. 15. The OPW for aqueous SCs are limited to ~1.23 V which constraints the improvement of energy density. In this case, they go above that limit by dint of the voltage drop in the hetero-structural region. The built-in electric field in the heterostructures of the composite is the prime reason for these improvements in the OPW and C sp .

Highly conductive nano-network and large SSA of C-dots have induced superior electrochemical performance of 189 F g−1 at 0.14 A g−1 when used with MnO2 with cycle stability up to 2000 cycles along with high energy density of 40 W h kg−1 and power density of 3385 W kg−1 [36].

In 2017, Xu et al. [37] have successfully synthesized C-dots/MnO2 composites where C-dots are homogeneously distributed onto the transparent petals of flower-like δ-MnO2. Consequently, a high SSA of 168.8 m2 g−1 has been achieved for the C-dots/MnO2 sample with 10 wt% C-dots (C-dots/MnO2-10). This is conducive to achieving high C sp. As a consequence, the values of C sp of 345, 333, 318, 306, and 296 F g−1 have been obtained at current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 3 and 4 A g−1, respectively, with good rate capability and excellent charge-discharge stability with 90.3% retention rate from its initial value after 10,000 cycles at 20 A g−1. All of these improvements can be attributed to the high SSA, high electrical conductivity and high hydrophilicity of GQDs.

GQDs, because of their smaller sizes, can be embedded between the interlayer spaces of 2D-TMO. GQDs have been embedded between the interlayer spacing of 2D-V2O5 nanosheets (VNS). Consequently, expansion of the interlayer spacing along with the increment of surface area has been achieved creating more charge storage sites for both EDLC and pseudocapacitive behaviors. Short diffusion path for ions and enhanced electrical conductivity endowed by the presence of GQDs allow fast electrochemical kinetics boosting the electrochemical performance of 2D-V2O5. Thus, the as-prepared VNS-GQD show C sp of 572 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 with 92% retention of its initial capacitance after 10,000 charge-discharge cycles [114].

Poor cycle stability and lower electrical conductivity restrict the practical use of layered double hydroxide (e.g., NiCo-LDH) despite their high theoretical C sp of 3000 F g−1 and outstanding redox activity [115,116]. Extending the crack propagation path and bridging the broken LDH nanosheets, GQDs can avoid sudden structural rupture. This improves the cycling stability of the material. GQDs can thus act as a toughening material in NiCo-LDH, increasing its cycle stability with capacitance retention of 79.2% after 5000 cycles under 20 A g−1. Moreover, they endow the hybrid with high electrical conductivity facilitating redox activities. As a result, 2D-NiCo-LDH/0D-GQD composite exhibits a very high C sp of 2220 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 with great cycle stability [117].

Moreover, C-dots can play the role of a mediator between a TMO and another carbon structure by reinforcing the bonding strength between them. This feature of C-dots have been exploited by Lv et al. [38] They have used C-dots as a bridge for connecting and strengthening the bonding affinities between MnO2 and graphene aerogel (GA). Moreover, the use of C-dots enhances the SSA of 144.5 m2 g−1, introduces pores in the diameter ranging from 3 to 10 nm, and ensures homogeneous distribution of MnO2 over GA, making more reaction sites available for the electrolytes. For these reasons, a high C sp of 721 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 has been achieved. Because of the reinforcement coming from C-dots, the as-prepared MnO2/C-dots/GA composite has been found to be very stable with improved cycle stability (92.3% capacitance retention after 10,000 at 10 A g−1) compared with MnO2/GA and MnO2/G.

Similarly, a conductive 3D-carbon matrix ornamented with homogeneously distributed Fe2O3 nanoparticles has been developed from a precursor mixture of small-size C-dots and small-size Fe2O3 nanoparticles upon CO2-laser conversion. The presence of C-dots has paved the path to enable a more homogeneous mixture of starting materials and as a result, a fine dispersion of small size Fe2O3 nanoparticles in the carbon matrix is ensured, which is a prerequisite for enabling a large number of surface faradaic reactions. By incorporating Fe2O3 nanoparticles homogeneously and exploiting the redox reactions involving Fe2O3, the areal capacitance has been boosted by nine-times compared to pure 3D turbostratic graphene [118].

Conducting polymers such as PANI [139] and polypyrrole (PPy) act as a promising electrode material for SC application. They contribute to the charge storage by acting as a pseudocapacitive material. They do so by dint of their high intrinsic electrical conductivity and large charge density [119,140,141]. Energy density of the conducting polymers can be further amplified by the aid of various TMOs with them [143]. But the main obstacle with them is their poor cycle stability which restricts their use as an effective electrode material. The effectiveness of conducting polymer can be improved by fusing C-dots with them.

Uniform distribution of C-dots over the surface of PANI was achieved by Mehare et al. [39]. Sucrose derived C-dots/ PANI (S-C-Dots/PANI) nanocomposite has been prepared by electrodeposition method. A C sp of 1512.4 F g−1 has been achieved at 1 A g−1 with S-C-dots/PANI-25 nanocomposite (25 signify the content of 25 mg sucrose) with excellent 76% capacitance retention at 3 A g−1 after 1000 cycles. High electrical conductivity due to the presence of S-C-dots gave rise to this high C sp and higher cycle stability.

Conducting polymer hydrogels (CPHs) are employed as an effective electrode material because of their 3D polymeric networks and inherent soft nature [120]. However, their use is limited because of poor electrical conductivity which can be cured by using C-dots. Improvement of electrical conductivity of CPH has been conducted by Li et al. [121] in their recent works. A facile synthesis route has been reported to fabricate soft PPy hydrogels with in situ doped edge sulfonated graphene quantum dots (sGQD). A higher C sp of 641 F g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 and excellent cycle stability compared with pristine PPy hydrogel have been achieved. This kind of superior electrochemical performance can be attributed to the activities of sGQD. First of all, protonation of the nitrogen of pyrrole is possible by the sulfonic acid groups situated on the surface of the sGQDs. This develops electrostatic interactions between the PPy chains that leads to the cross-linking of the chains forming a 3D polymeric structure that can be gelated into a hydrogel. Moreover, sGQDs form transport channels for fast solvated ions diffusion during charge and discharge. All of these, along with the high electrical conductivity of sGQDs have improved the C sp of PPy hydrogels.

High SSA with numerous active sites on GQDs has improved the electrochemical performance of PPy. PPy/C-dots composite or PCD has been synthesized by one-step electro-polymerization technique in an aqueous solution. Modification of PPy with C-dots has provided the composite with high electrical conductivity. This is reflected from the reduction of charge transfer resistance of the PPy from 2.7 to 0.11 Ω. As a result, a high C sp of 838 F g−1 at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 with mass loading 1.3 g cm−2 has been achieved with PCD-0.5 (PPy: C-dots as 95:05 w/w). Moreover, PCD-30 (PCD-0.5 with electropolymerization time 30 min) demonstrates a very high C sp of 1073 F g−1 at 10 m V s−1 with mass loading 1.4 g cm−2 with cyclic stability 62% even after 2000 cycles [40].

At the end, we can infer that C-dots have some unique, noteworthy, and impressive characteristics that make it more competent compared with other materials. It has greater potential to be employed as an electrode material. As discussed above, improved supercapacitive activities of various materials have been resulted from the utilization of C-dots within their matrix. A table ( Table 5) and a scheme ( Fig. 16) are given below summarizing the greater essence of C-dots in SC application.

In this review, a deep insight on the fundamental knowledge-base of the use of C-dots as active materials for conversion of solar energy, water-splitting catalyst, and materials of choice for storage of energy in supercapacitors has been established (Fig. 16). There are indeed, many challenges in designing and application of materials for practical applications. Some of the challenges, as reviewed extensively, can be met by exploiting the inherent properties of C-dots. These materials may be prepared as thin films and properties including morphology may be tuned for realizing practical applications with superior performances. Special care must be taken regarding synthetic routes and purification to avoid probable undesirable and inconsistent performances from impurities [144]. Precursors should also be preferably renewable. Judicious selection of additives during synthesis is also crucial to improve the properties of C-dots for improved performances. Specific challenges in designing the materials with focus on C-dots in different fields are summarized below ( Fig. 17).

The materials with narrow spectral absorption and electron-hole recombination lead to poor performance of DSSCs. Another problem associated with photovoltaic devices is that they can only realize electricity generation under sunlight illumination, and the power generation is low or zero in dark-light conditions. Therefore, a challenging but promising task is to realize persistent electricity generation in multi-weather conditions. Recently, bifunctional DSSCs tailored by graphene, graphene based coating films, and platinum alloys have been realized to harvest solar and rain energies. C-dots appear promising to meet the challenges.

Although the tunability of the electron density of C-dots by doping with other elements such as B and P atoms has been well explored in SC applications, the concept is yet to be exploited for fabrication of anode of DSSCs. Surface heteroatom doping in C-dots with long chain molecule deteriorates their performance as long chain functions as a carrier insulator. Hence, while introducing a surface heteroatom a successful red shifted absorption pattern should be ensured while not impeding the carrier mobility. Furthermore, the study of the effects of different forms of PANI such as leucoemeraldine, emeraldine and pernigraniline in composites formed with undoped and doped C-dots for use as CE of DSSCs is in the very rudimentary stage and it would be an important field of future study.

One of the most important properties of catalysts in photochemical production of hydrogen is the stability against the rigorous conditions such as presence of electromagnetic radiation, reactive reaction intermediates, local pH and mechanical knocking by the bubble formed. In addition, sizable energy gap of the catalyst where clusters with smaller size increases energy gap are important for operating the water splitting reaction with high rate and efficiency. The redox potentials of C-dots are easily affected by the presence of impurities and therefore, a rigorous purification becomes necessary during synthesis. As a member of the family of highly stable elements, C-dots would exhibit high tolerance against the factors associated with destruction of catalysts. Moreover, it is possible to fabricate C-dots easily with desired particle size that possess energy gap well-fitted with the desired operational electromagnetic radiation. Incorporation of C-dots into other carbonaceous materials may increase the mechanical stability and catalytic activity of the composite resulting in the advancement in H2 evolution via water splitting process [145].

Electrical energy in supercapacitors is stored in a capacitive double layer at the interfaces between two high-surface-area electrodes and an electrolyte. The energy and power performance of a supercapacitor are evaluated by three metrics: capacitance, voltage window, and equivalent series resistance. In addition, the retention of charges with repeated cycling uses and kinetics of charge storage determines the cycling life of a device. As stated earlier, during the synthesis of C-dots, the as-prepared mixtures contain impurities, which depending on the sizes, doping ratio and surface may affect electrochemical properties and therefore a rigorous purification becomes necessary. On the other hand, electrochemical properties of C-dots may be modulated by deliberate but careful addition of suitable additive/pre-cursors during synthesis. C-dots exhibit high intrinsic conductivity and the energy stored in the electric double layer is rapidly available with high reversibility, leading to high power density and cycling stability for overall capacitive storage. On tackling the limited energy density by tuning pore size into the size regime of desolvated electrolyte ions to minimize diffusion resistances would be possible with C-dots.

This review has been a unique attempt to bring researchers devoted to energy research in a common venue to polish and enrich their knowledge regarding the possibility of preparation of C-dots-based materials with tunable properties by overseeing the literatures of three promising fields discoursed in a single platform. Through extensive survey of literature, the current status of the use of C-dots for energy conversion and storage have been identified. The progress of ongoing global research activities on this particular issue concludes that C-dots are in real sense the energy materials for future energy devices and further researches should be conducted with emphasis on C-dots to resolve the global energy crisis.
